primary_diagnosis.name: GI mast cell activation flare with volume depletion — surgical abdomen and anaphylaxis to be excluded

pathophysiology_summary: Gut mast cell degranulation after a food trigger releases histamine and mediators causing intestinal smooth-muscle spasm, hypermotility, secretory diarrhoea, distension and flushing, with large fluid shifts producing tachycardia and near-syncope. This mimics a surgical abdomen (obstruction/appendicitis/ischaemia) and gastroenteritis. Reassuring features are a soft non-peritonitic abdomen, no GI bleeding, pain relief after motions, and reproducible food triggers. However, the food-triggered combination of flushing, GI symptoms and near-syncope means anaphylaxis must be screened, and the heavy fluid loss with tachycardia and isolation creates a genuine dehydration/collapse risk. Telehealth cannot examine the abdomen, measure BP or give IV fluids, so the correct action is urgent in-person/ED assessment for abdominal evaluation and IV rehydration, an anaphylaxis screen/adrenaline plan, and no remote injections.

differential.evidence_basis: Post-food cramps/distension/diarrhoea with flushing on a known MCAS background.
Severe pain and distension demand exclusion of a surgical cause.
Food-triggered flushing + GI + near-syncope can be anaphylaxis; screen airway/circulation.
Post-food rapid onset with chronic similar flares and no sick contacts is against infection but consider it.

differential.diagnosis: GI mast cell activation flare with dehydration
differential.evidence_basis: Soft abdomen, no bleeding, pain relief after motions, food trigger — a gut flare, but with significant volume depletion.
No airway features, but near-syncope after food keeps an anaphylactic component in play — safety-net and ensure adrenaline.
Lower probability but requires in-person confirmation.
Immediate post-food onset with reproducible food triggers.

differential.diagnosis: GI mast cell activation flare with volume depletion
differential.evidence_basis: Working telehealth position: gut mast cell flare with dehydration needing urgent in-person assessment (abdominal review + IV fluids), with anaphylaxis screened and an adrenaline plan ensured.

red_flags.description: Near-syncope/faintness with tachycardia and heavy fluid loss (volume depletion ± anaphylactic circulatory component)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Indicates significant dehydration and/or an anaphylactic circulatory component requiring urgent assessment/IV fluids (± adrenaline).

red_flags.description: Airway/breathing involvement (throat tightness, voice change, wheeze)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would confirm anaphylaxis needing adrenaline and 000.

red_flags.description: Rigid board-like abdomen, GI bleeding, or inability to pass urine
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate a surgical abdomen or severe complication.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing everything to MCAS and missing a surgical abdomen, significant dehydration, or an anaphylactic component.
Treating food-triggered flushing + GI + near-syncope as purely 'localised' without screening/adrenaline planning.
Arranging IM hyoscine/ondansetron over chat or prolonged fasting instead of assessment/rehydration.
Advising the patient to ride it out at home despite near-syncope, tachycardia and isolation.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Clinically dehydrated with tachycardia/near-syncope; anaphylaxis screen without airway features but food-triggered — assess in person
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Supports urgent in-person/ED assessment with IV fluids and anaphylaxis vigilance.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Blood pressure/postural BP and objective hydration status
IV rehydration and bloods (U&E, FBC, CRP, lactate, tryptase)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Screen anaphylaxis and surgical features; arrange urgent in-person/ED assessment for an abdominal review and IV rehydration; escalate to adrenaline + 000 for airway/breathing/collapse, and to ED for a rigid abdomen or GI bleeding.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Managing this remotely as a 'gut flare' could miss a surgical abdomen, an anaphylactic component, or leave a dehydrated isolated patient to collapse.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Food-triggered reactions with GI plus circulatory features can meet anaphylaxis criteria; adrenaline is first-line and patients at risk should carry an autoinjector.
Severe abdominal pain with systemic features requires in-person assessment to exclude a surgical cause and to manage dehydration.
